text.title: `trivy fs` + `trivy image` Blocking Gate
text.summary: PRs that introduce HIGH or CRITICAL CVEs in dependencies, base images, or IaC misconfigs fail the required check; allowlist additions require a sunset date.

section.title: Two scan modes, one gate
section.p: `trivy fs` covers source-tree dependencies and IaC files; `trivy image` covers built containers. Both run on every PR and the merge gate is the union — a vulnerability discovered in either mode blocks merge.

rule.statement: Each PR MUST run `trivy fs --severity HIGH,CRITICAL --exit-code 1 .` against the source tree and, when the PR builds a container, `trivy image --severity HIGH,CRITICAL --exit-code 1 ` against the built artifact. Either non-zero exit MUST block merge. CVE allowlist entries in `.trivyignore` MUST include a one-line justification, an owner, and a `# sunset:YYYY-MM-DD` comment; CI MUST fail if any allowlist entry is past its sunset.
rule.rationale: Filesystem-only scans miss vulnerabilities introduced by the base image; image-only scans miss source-level CVEs in tooling that never lands in the container. Running both is cheap (seconds in cache) and closes both gaps. Sunset dates prevent the allowlist from becoming a graveyard of forgotten exceptions.

example.code: trivy fs --severity HIGH,CRITICAL --exit-code 1 \ --ignorefile .trivyignore . trivy image --severity HIGH,CRITICAL --exit-code 1 \ --ignorefile .trivyignore $IMAGE_REF
example.note: Same allowlist, both modes, both gates.
`--exit-code 0` reduces the scan to a logging step; the gate is informational, not enforcing.

antipattern.description: Adding a CVE to `.trivyignore` with the comment "noisy" and no owner.
antipattern.reason: The allowlist becomes a perpetual cover; require owner + sunset so review is unavoidable.
